title: Opening Address at the 13th WTO Ministerial Conference

paragraphs.text: Your Excellencies, Ministers, distinguished delegates, ladies and gentlemen. Welcome to Abu Dhabi for the Thirteenth Ministerial Conference of the World Trade Organization. I want to begin by expressing our gratitude to the Government of the United Arab Emirates for hosting this conference and for the extraordinary preparations that have gone into making this gathering possible.

We meet at a time of considerable turbulence in the global economy. The aftershocks of the pandemic, the disruptions caused by geopolitical conflicts, climate-related disasters, and persistent inflationary pressures have tested the resilience of the multilateral trading system as never before. Yet trade continues to serve as a lifeline for billions of people around the world, and the WTO remains the indispensable institution for governing it.

Let me share some numbers that underscore what is at stake. Global merchandise trade reached approximately nineteen trillion dollars last year, while commercial services trade exceeded seven trillion. Behind these figures are farmers, factory workers, small business owners, and entrepreneurs whose livelihoods depend on open, predictable, and fair trade rules. When we succeed here in Abu Dhabi, we succeed for them.

The most pressing deliverable before us is the conclusion of negotiations on fisheries subsidies. At MC12 in Geneva, we achieved a historic first agreement banning subsidies for illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing. But we left unfinished the provisions on subsidies contributing to overcapacity and overfishing. The science is unambiguous: without disciplines on these subsidies, we risk the collapse of fish stocks that feed over three billion people worldwide.

I urge all members to show the flexibility needed to close this deal. The fisheries subsidies agreement is not just a trade matter — it is an environmental imperative and a development priority. Small-scale artisanal fishers in developing countries, who account for the vast majority of fishing employment, stand to benefit the most from sustainable fisheries management. We owe it to them and to future generations.

On agriculture, we must make progress on longstanding issues that matter deeply to developing and least-developed countries. Food security has moved from the margins to the center of the global agenda. The disruptions in grain exports caused by the conflict in Ukraine demonstrated how quickly supply shocks can translate into hunger for the most vulnerable populations. Our rules must reflect this reality.

We need to address the issue of public stockholding for food security purposes in a way that respects the legitimate concerns of developing countries while maintaining the integrity of our trade disciplines. We also need concrete outcomes on domestic support, market access, and export competition. The agricultural negotiations have been stalled for too long, and the cost of inaction falls disproportionately on the poorest.

The dispute settlement system is the backbone of the WTO, and its restoration must be a priority for this conference. For over four years, the Appellate Body has been non-functional, and the credibility of our organization depends on our ability to resolve disputes in a binding, two-stage process. Members have engaged constructively in the reform discussions, and I believe a pathway to a fully functioning system is within reach if political will is mobilized.

Let me be candid: a WTO without a functioning dispute settlement mechanism is like a legal system without an appeals court. Rules without enforcement become suggestions, and suggestions do not provide the predictability that businesses and governments need. I call on all members to demonstrate the leadership required to reach agreement on dispute settlement reform at this ministerial.

The digital economy presents both enormous opportunities and new challenges for the trading system. E-commerce now accounts for a rapidly growing share of global transactions, yet our rulebook was written before the internet era. The Joint Initiative on E-Commerce has produced valuable discussions on data flows, electronic signatures, consumer protection, and cybersecurity. We must find a way to translate these discussions into binding commitments that keep the WTO relevant in the digital age.

At the same time, I am acutely conscious that the digital divide remains wide. Many developing countries and least-developed countries lack the digital infrastructure, skills, and regulatory frameworks to fully participate in digital trade. Any digital trade rules we develop must include robust provisions for technical assistance, capacity building, and special and differential treatment to ensure that the digital economy is inclusive.

The moratorium on customs duties on electronic transmissions has been a subject of intense debate. Some developing countries are concerned about foregone revenue, while others point to the benefits of duty-free digital trade for their consumers and businesses. I encourage members to approach this issue pragmatically and to consider creative solutions that address revenue concerns without stifling digital development.

On WTO reform more broadly, we have made significant strides since MC12. The reform agenda encompasses the deliberative function of the organization, its monitoring and transparency activities, and the development dimension of our work. We must ensure that reform strengthens the WTO's ability to address twenty-first century challenges — including climate change, pandemic preparedness, and industrial policy — while preserving the core principles of non-discrimination and transparency.

Trade and environmental sustainability can and must be mutually reinforcing. The growing fragmentation of environmental regulations across markets creates unnecessary barriers, particularly for developing country exporters. We need dialogue on carbon border adjustments, environmental goods and services liberalization, and the circular economy. The WTO must be part of the climate solution, not a bystander.

I want to say a special word about least-developed countries. The LDCs continue to face enormous structural challenges, and their share of global trade remains stubbornly low at under one percent. The smooth transition framework for graduating LDCs, the Enhanced Integrated Framework, and duty-free quota-free market access commitments are essential elements of our development agenda. We must deliver for the most vulnerable members of our organization.

The accession of new members to the WTO is another important agenda item. The ongoing accession negotiations with Comoros, Timor-Leste, and other applicants demonstrate the continued relevance and attractiveness of the multilateral trading system. Each new member strengthens the universality of our rules and expands the benefits of trade to more people.

Ministers, I am sometimes asked whether the WTO is still relevant in an era of geopolitical rivalry and unilateral action. My answer is emphatic: the WTO has never been more relevant. Precisely because the world is fragmented, we need a forum where all members — large and small, developed and developing — can negotiate rules, resolve disputes, and hold each other accountable. The alternative to multilateral trade governance is not freedom; it is the law of the jungle.

But relevance is not automatic. It must be earned through results. If we leave Abu Dhabi without meaningful outcomes, we will have squandered an opportunity and deepened skepticism about multilateral cooperation. The global public is watching, and they are judging us not by our speeches but by our capacity to deliver agreements that make a tangible difference in people's lives.

I have seen what is possible when members engage with ambition and pragmatism. The TRIPS waiver decision, the fisheries subsidies first pillar, the services domestic regulation agreement — these achievements of MC12 prove that the WTO can deliver when political will is present. Let us summon that will again here in Abu Dhabi.

In closing, I want to thank the chairs of our negotiating groups for their tireless efforts, and I want to thank each of you for your commitment to this process. The next few days will be demanding. The negotiations will be complex. But the stakes are too high for us to accept anything less than success. Let us get to work. Thank you.
